scene.title: Steel cabin with loft & porch
scene.description: 6×5 m steel cabin, 3.12 m walls (30% taller), rear loft at 3.25 m, 2 m deep front porch. Schematic steel construction with metallic grays — not load-designed. Roof eaves seated on walls; porch roof slopes away from cabin.

objects.label: Loft floor
objects.note: Rear steel loft platform at 3.25 m

objects.label: Ladder rail L
objects.note: Steel ladder stile to taller loft

objects.label: Roof left pitch
objects.note: Steel roof plate ~25°; eave ~3.4 m, peak ~5.1 m

objects.label: Roof right pitch
objects.note: Mirror pitch; seats on taller wall top

objects.label: Ridge board
objects.note: Steel ridge at pitch intersection ~5.1 m

objects.label: Porch post FL
objects.note: y = H/2 on deck; steel post ×1.3 height

objects.label: Porch roof
objects.note: Slopes down toward −Z (away from cabin); rot X = −12°

objects.label: Gable fill L
objects.note: Steel gable trim on taller pitch

objects.label: Chimney
objects.note: Weathered steel stack (decorative); raised with taller roof

messages.content:
👋 I'm **Grok** — your co-builder.

We design **in 3D together**. Tell me anything you can imagine:
- *“Build a steel pedestrian bridge”*
- *“Make a 6-DOF robot arm”*
- *“Design a tiny Mars habitat”*
- *“A wooden cabin with a loft”*

I'll place real geometry you can orbit, and **we can change it anytime**. I remember everything we build and talk about.

What should we create first?
Design a wooden cabin with a loft and porch

Built a **cozy wooden cabin with a loft and front porch** — simple structural parts, realistic proportions.

### Layout (defaults I assumed)
| Part | Size |
|------|------|
| Cabin footprint | **6 m × 5 m** |
| Wall height (eave) | **2.4 m** |
| Loft floor | rear half, at **2.5 m** |
| Porch | **6 m × 2 m**, ~2.4 m posts |
| Gable roof peak | ~**4.0 m** |

### What’s in the model
- Raised timber **floor** + **porch deck**
- Board walls with a **front door** and side **windows**
- Four porch **posts** and a lightly sloped **porch roof**
- **Gable roof** (two pitches) over the cabin
- **Loft platform** at the rear with a safety rail
- Simple **ladder** up to the loft
- Warm wood tones throughout (schematic, not load-designed)

Orbit around — porch faces −Z. Want it wider, a stone chimney, bigger loft, or glass French doors next?
Change primary materials to steel with metallic gray colors. Update the 3D scene.

Converted the cabin to a **steel schematic** — same layout and proportions, metallic gray palette.

### Material update
| Element | Before | After |
|---------|--------|-------|
| Structure (walls, floors, posts, beams) | Warm timber | **Steel grays** (`#6b7280`–`#9ca3af`) |
| Roof | Dark wood | **Darker steel plate** (`#4b5563`) |
| Door / accents | Brown wood | **Charcoal steel** (`#374151`) |
| Windows | Soft blue glass | Kept translucent blue-gray glass |
| Chimney | Stone-look | **Weathered steel stack** |

### Material params
- **metalness** ≈ 0.75–0.9 on primary structure
- **roughness** ≈ 0.25–0.4 (brushed / plate steel feel)

Geometry is unchanged: **6×5 m** footprint, **2.4 m** walls, loft at **2.5 m**, **2 m** porch facing −Z.

Still schematic — not a structural design. Want galvanized vs painted steel, corrugated roof panels, or I-beam style members next?

Good eye — the roof had a few real geometry bugs, not just a style choice. I fixed them in the scene.

### What was wrong

1. **Porch roof sloped the wrong way**  
   It was rotated `+12°` around X, so the **outer edge was higher** and the cabin side was lower. Water (and the visual read) would drain **toward the door**, not off the porch. Note said “slope away from cabin,” but the rotation did the opposite.

2. **Main roof sat too high on the walls**  
   Wall tops are at **y = 2.4 m**. With centers at **y = 3.3** and **25°** pitch, the eaves floated around **~2.56 m** — a visible gap above the steel panels.

3. **Ridge slightly off the pitch intersection**  
   Left/right plates met near **~4.0 m**, while the ridge beam sat a bit low/misaligned relative to that meet line.

### What I changed
| Part | Fix |
|------|-----|
| **Porch roof** | Rotation **`[−12, 0, 0]`** so it falls away from the cabin; seat adjusted onto the porch header |
| **Main pitches** | Centers lowered to **y ≈ 3.18** so eaves land ~**2.45 m** on the walls |
| **Ridge** | Raised/aligned to **~3.92 m** at the true peak |
| **Gable trim** | Tweaked to follow the corrected pitch line |

Same layout: **6×5 m** cabin, **2.4 m** walls, **2 m** porch, ~**25°** gable. Still schematic steel — not load-designed.

Orbit the front/porch: you should see a clean fall-away porch roof and eaves sitting on the walls. Want **corrugated panels**, deeper overhangs, or a **gutters + downspouts** pass next?
why is the roof like that

Good question — the roof looks the way it does on purpose. Here’s the short structural logic.

### Two different roofs, two jobs

| Roof | Shape | Why |
|------|--------|-----|
| **Main cabin** | **Gable** (two plates, ±25° about Z) | Classic shed form: water runs left/right off the long sides, ridge at center |
| **Porch** | **Lean-to** (one plate, −12° about X) | Simple cover over the deck; water runs **away from the door** toward −Z |

They meet the cabin differently, so they *shouldn’t* look the same.

---

### Main gable — why it sits like that

Wall tops are at **y ≈ 2.4 m**. Each steel pitch is a flat plate rotated in Z:

- **Left pitch:** `rot Z = +25°`, center near **x = −1.55**, **y ≈ 3.18**
- **Right pitch:** `rot Z = −25°`, center near **x = +1.55**, **y ≈ 3.18**
- **Ridge** at the intersection: **y ≈ 3.92 m**

Geometry rule we use:

```
eave ≈ wall top  (≈ 2.45 m)
peak ≈ eave + (half-span × tan 25°)
     ≈ 2.45 + (1.55 × 0.466) ≈ 3.17… → centers mid-height; ridge ~3.9 m
```

So the “tent” shape isn’t random — **eaves seat on the walls**, plates meet at the ridge. That’s why it looks steep in the middle and low at the sides.

---

### Porch roof — why it’s tilted the other way

Porch faces **−Z**. For a plate whose thickness is local **Y** and depth is along **Z**:

- **`rot X = +12°`** → outer edge **up** → drains **toward the door** (wrong)
- **`rot X = −12°`** → outer edge **down** → drains **off the porch** (correct)

Header under the inner edge is at **y ≈ 2.45 m**; the plate center sits a bit higher (**y ≈ 2.7**) so that after the tilt, the cabin side still reads as seated and the −Z edge drops.

---

### Why it can still look “odd”

1. **Schematic plates**, not corrugated sheets or rafters — so you see two big slabs + one porch slab.
2. **Gable is side-to-side** (X); **porch slopes front-to-back** (Z) — different axes on purpose.
3. **Chimney / loft** poke through the volume; they’re decorative, not sealed flashing details.
4. **Not load-designed** — proportions only (25° / 12° are visual, not code).

Orbit front (−Z): porch should fall **away** from the door. Orbit side: gable should rise to ~**3.9 m** at center.

Want it to *look* different next — shallower pitch, deeper eaves, single mono-pitch whole cabin, or rafters + purlins instead of solid plates?
Make it 30 percent taller. Update the 3D scene.

Made the steel cabin **30% taller** — footprint stays **6×5 m**, porch still **2 m** deep; only heights and vertical placements scale.

### Height scale (×1.3)
| Element | Before | After |
|---------|--------|--------|
| Wall height | 2.4 m | **3.12 m** |
| Wall tops / eaves | ~2.45–2.6 m | **~3.4 m** |
| Door | 2.0 m | **2.6 m** |
| Loft floor | 2.5 m | **3.25 m** |
| Gable ridge / peak | ~3.9 m | **~5.1 m** |
| Porch posts | 2.2 m | **2.86 m** |
| Porch header | 2.45 m | **3.19 m** |

### What stayed the same
- Plan layout: cabin, loft at rear, porch toward **−Z**
- Roof logic: gable **±25° about Z**, porch lean-to **−12° about X** (away from the door)
- Steel palette and metalness / roughness
- Schematic build — not load-designed

Gable plates reseated so eaves still land on the taller walls; ridge follows the new peak. Camera target lifted so the whole mass still frames well.

Want **taller only the loft**, a **second storey**, or **same height but steeper roof** next?
